prompt: The word kecantikan, from cantik (beautiful), is...
opt_a_why: Correct - ke- and -an make the abstract noun kecantikan.
opt_b_why: That would need a different affix.
opt_c_why: cantik on its own is the adjective.
opt_d: a doer noun meaning a beautiful person
opt_d_why: ke- and -an do not make a doer.
explanation: ke-...-an turns an adjective into an abstract noun: kecantikan means beauty.

prompt: What does pendidikan mean?
opt_a_why: Correct - pe- and -an on didik give the noun pendidikan.
opt_b_why: To educate is mendidik.
opt_c_why: That is an adjective idea, not this noun.
opt_d_why: A teacher is guru or pendidik.
explanation: pe-...-an makes a process noun: pendidikan means education.

prompt: The word perkahwinan comes from kahwin (to marry) and means...
opt_a_why: Correct - per- and -an form the noun perkahwinan.
opt_b_why: To marry is berkahwin.
opt_c_why: That is an adjective idea.
opt_d_why: per- and -an do not mark tense.
explanation: per-...-an, built from ber- verbs, makes nouns: perkahwinan means marriage.

prompt: What does kehujanan mean?
opt_a_why: Correct - ke- and -an can mean being caught by something.
opt_b_why: That is musim hujan.
opt_c_why: To rain is just hujan.
opt_d_why: Heavy rain is hujan lebat.
explanation: ke-...-an also forms adversative nouns: kehujanan means caught in the rain.

prompt: Make the abstract noun meaning safety, from selamat, using ke- and -an
explanation: selamat wrapped in ke- and -an gives keselamatan - safety.

prompt: What is the passive of Saya membaca buku itu?
opt_a_why: Correct - the me- verb becomes di-, and the doer follows oleh.
opt_b_why: This says the book read me.
opt_c_why: The subject and object are mixed up.
opt_d_why: This is not a passive sentence.
explanation: The di- passive: Buku itu dibaca oleh saya.

prompt: In a di- passive sentence, the doer is introduced by which word?
opt_a_why: Correct - oleh marks the agent: dibaca oleh saya.
opt_b_why: yang links a noun to a description.
opt_c_why: di is a place preposition or the passive prefix itself.
opt_d_why: ke means to a place.
explanation: oleh introduces the agent in a passive sentence.

prompt: What does Dia kena marah mean?
opt_a_why: Correct - kena makes an adversative passive.
opt_b_why: That would be the active Dia memarahi.
opt_c_why: kena does not mark the future.
opt_d_why: That would be Dia marah.
explanation: kena forms an adversative passive - something unpleasant happened to the subject.

prompt: In Buku itu saya baca, the verb baca appears...
opt_a_why: Correct - the pronoun-fronted passive drops me-.
opt_b_why: Malay has no tense.
opt_c_why: baca is still a verb here.
opt_d_why: This pattern is correct and very common.
explanation: When the doer is a pronoun, Malay drops me- and oleh: Buku itu saya baca.

prompt: Fill the blank with the passive of wrote (root tulis): Surat itu ___ oleh dia.
explanation: The di- passive of tulis is ditulis.

prompt: The colloquial word tak is the everyday form of which standard word?
opt_a_why: Correct - tak is the spoken short form of tidak.
opt_b_why: The short form of tetapi is tapi.
opt_c_why: tahu means to know.
opt_d_why: tentu means certain.
explanation: tak is the colloquial form of tidak (not).

prompt: In casual speech, nak is short for which standard word?
opt_a_why: Correct - nak is the spoken form of hendak (want to).
opt_b_why: makan means to eat.
opt_c_why: The short form of tidak is tak.
opt_d_why: boleh means can.
explanation: nak is the colloquial form of hendak (to want).

prompt: In a formal news report, which form should you write?
opt_a_why: Correct - formal writing uses the standard form tidak.
opt_b_why: tak is colloquial, not for formal writing.
opt_c_why: x is a texting abbreviation.
opt_d_why: dak is a dialect form.
explanation: Formal writing uses bahasa baku - the standard tidak, not tak.

prompt: The colloquial saja or je is the everyday form of which word?
opt_a_why: Correct - saja and je are spoken forms of sahaja (only).
opt_b_why: saya means I.
opt_c_why: sama means same.
opt_d_why: soal means question or issue.
explanation: sahaja (only, just) becomes saja or je in speech.

prompt: The particle -kah marks...
opt_a_why: Correct - -kah turns a word into a question, common in writing.
opt_b_why: That is closer to -lah.
opt_c_why: Malay has no tense.
opt_d_why: -kah does not mark number.
explanation: -kah marks a question: Betulkah? means Is that right?

prompt: In Pergilah!, what does the -lah particle do?
opt_a: it softens or emphasises the command
opt_a_why: Correct - -lah adds emphasis or a gentle nudge.
opt_b_why: Malay has no tense.
opt_c_why: That is the -kah particle.
opt_d_why: Negation uses tidak or jangan.
explanation: -lah softens or emphasises: Pergilah! means Do go.

prompt: In Saya pun tahu, what does pun mean?
opt_a_why: Correct - pun adds the sense of too or even.
opt_b_why: not is tidak.
opt_c_why: very is sangat.
opt_d_why: never is tidak pernah.
explanation: pun means too or even: Saya pun tahu means I know too.

prompt: In the word rumahnya, what does the -nya add?
opt_a: the meaning his, its or the
opt_a_why: Correct - -nya works as a possessive or a definite marker.
opt_b_why: my is saya, placed after the noun.
opt_c_why: a question would be the -kah particle.
opt_d_why: -nya does not negate.
explanation: -nya means his, its or the: rumahnya means his house or the house.

prompt: Which noun correctly pairs the affix with its root and meaning?
opt_a_why: Correct - pe-...-an on bangun forms the process noun pembangunan.
opt_b_why: A builder is pembangun; ke-...-an would not mean builder.
opt_c_why: perbangun is not a word; the form is pembangunan.
opt_d: bangunan from bangun - the act of waking
opt_d_why: bangunan means a building, not the act of waking.
explanation: pe-...-an makes process nouns: pembangunan means development.

prompt: Both kebersihan and pembersihan come from bersih. How do they differ?
opt_a: kebersihan is the quality cleanliness; pembersihan is the process of cleaning
opt_a_why: Correct - ke-...-an gives an abstract quality, pe-...-an gives a process.
opt_b: they mean exactly the same thing
opt_b_why: They are not interchangeable; one is a state, one is a process.
opt_c: kebersihan is the process; pembersihan is the quality
opt_c_why: This reverses the two; ke-...-an is the quality.
opt_d: both are verbs meaning to clean
opt_d_why: To clean is membersihkan; both words here are nouns.
explanation: ke-...-an = abstract quality (kebersihan, cleanliness); pe-...-an = process (pembersihan, cleansing).

prompt: Which sentence uses the nominalised form correctly?
opt_a_why: Correct - kejayaan (success) is the right ke-...-an noun and fits the subject slot.
opt_b_why: jaya is the bare root; the noun success here should be kejayaan.
opt_c_why: penjayaan is not the standard noun for success.
opt_d_why: berjaya is the verb to succeed, not the noun the sentence needs.
explanation: kejayaan is the abstract noun success, formed with ke-...-an from jaya.

prompt: What does penyelesaian, from selesai, mean?
opt_a_why: Correct - pe-...-an on selesai gives penyelesaian, a solution or settlement.
opt_b_why: finished is simply selesai.
opt_c_why: to solve is menyelesaikan.
opt_d_why: a problem is masalah, not penyelesaian.
explanation: pe-...-an forms penyelesaian (a solution); the verb is menyelesaikan.

prompt: Form the abstract noun for honesty from jujur using ke- and -an: ___ amat penting dalam perniagaan.
explanation: jujur (honest) becomes kejujuran (honesty) with ke-...-an.

prompt: Form the noun from latih (train) using the suffix -an: Program ___ pekerja baharu bermula esok.
explanation: latih takes -an to form latihan (training); the new-staff training programme.

prompt: Translate to Malay: Government corruption slows down a country's progress.
accepted: rasuah kerajaan melambatkan kemajuan sesebuah negara|rasuah kerajaan melambatkan kemajuan negara
explanation: kemajuan (progress) is the ke-...-an noun from maju.

prompt: Arrange: penting / pendidikan / untuk / sangat / masa / depan
accepted: pendidikan sangat penting untuk masa depan
explanation: pendidikan (education) is the pe-...-an noun from didik; the sentence means education is very important for the future.

prompt: Which word correctly means cleverness, the abstract noun from pandai?
opt_a_why: Correct - ke-...-an on pandai gives kepandaian, cleverness.
opt_b_why: pemandaian is not a standard word.
opt_c_why: perpandaian is not a standard word.
opt_d_why: berpandai is not the noun; the noun is kepandaian.
explanation: ke-...-an turns the adjective pandai into the abstract noun kepandaian.

prompt: Which sentence is a correctly formed di- passive?
opt_a_why: Correct - dibina is the di- passive of membina, with oleh marking the agent.
opt_b_why: membina is the active verb; a passive needs di-.
opt_c_why: oleh must come before the agent, not after it.
opt_d_why: The roles are reversed; this says the government was built by the bridge.
explanation: The di- passive: subject + di-verb + oleh + agent.

prompt: Which sentence contains a mistake?
opt_a: Surat itu ditulis oleh dia semalam dengan cepat sekali.
opt_a_why: This is fine; ditulis oleh dia is a correct di- passive.
opt_b_why: Mistake - with a pronoun agent, Malay uses the pronoun-fronted passive saya beli, not di- + bare pronoun.
opt_c: Rumah itu dijual oleh mereka tahun lepas.
opt_c_why: This is correct; dijual oleh mereka is a proper di- passive.
opt_d_why: This is correct; dimakan oleh kucing is a proper di- passive.
explanation: With a pronoun agent the natural form is Buku itu saya beli, not Buku itu dibeli saya.

prompt: Which passive best fits a neutral news report: The thief was arrested by the police?
opt_a_why: Correct - the di- passive is the neutral, formal choice for reports.
opt_b_why: kena gives an adversative, colloquial tone unsuitable for a neutral report.
opt_c_why: Pronoun-fronted passive needs a pronoun; polis is a full noun, so this is wrong.
opt_d_why: This is active and reverses the meaning - the thief arrested the police.
explanation: The di- passive is the neutral register; kena adds an adversative, informal colour.

prompt: What is the difference between Dia dipukul and Dia kena pukul?
opt_a: Both mean he was hit, but kena pukul adds an adversative, colloquial tone
opt_a_why: Correct - di- is neutral; kena highlights misfortune and is more colloquial.
opt_b: dipukul is colloquial while kena pukul is formal
opt_b_why: It is the reverse; di- is the neutral or formal form.
opt_c_why: dipukul is passive - he was hit, not he hit.
opt_d: kena pukul means he will hit
opt_d_why: kena does not mark the future; it marks an adversative passive.
explanation: di- passive is neutral; kena passive stresses that something unpleasant befell the subject.

prompt: Translate to Malay (use the di- passive): The letter was sent by the manager yesterday.
accepted: surat itu dihantar oleh pengurus semalam
explanation: dihantar is the di- passive of menghantar; oleh marks the agent pengurus.

prompt: Translate to Malay using the pronoun-fronted passive: That book, I have already read.
accepted: buku itu sudah saya baca|buku itu telah saya baca
explanation: With a pronoun agent, the verb drops me-: buku itu saya baca.

prompt: Complete with the di- passive of makan: Nasi itu sudah ___ oleh mereka.
explanation: dimakan is the di- passive of makan; the rice was already eaten by them.

prompt: Arrange: oleh / itu / dibaiki / kereta / mekanik
explanation: dibaiki is the di- passive of membaiki; the sentence means the car was repaired by the mechanic.

prompt: Which sentence is written in correct formal register?
opt_a: Mesyuarat itu tidak dapat diteruskan kerana ketiadaan kuorum.
opt_a_why: Correct - tidak, diteruskan and kerana are all standard formal forms.
opt_b: Mesyuarat tu tak boleh sambung sebab takde orang.
opt_b_why: tu, tak, sebab and takde are all colloquial - unsuitable for formal writing.
opt_c: Mesyuarat itu x dapat diteruskan sebab takde kuorum.
opt_c_why: x and takde are colloquial or texting forms, not formal Malay.
opt_d: Mesyuarat tu tidak dapat diteruskan kerana ketiadaan kuorum.
opt_d_why: tu should be itu in formal register; the rest is fine.
explanation: Formal register uses itu, tidak, kerana and full me-/di- verbs, not colloquial tu, tak, sebab.

prompt: A friend texts you. Which reply sounds naturally colloquial?
opt_a: Aku tak dapat datang la, ada hal sikit.
opt_a_why: Correct - aku, tak, la and ada hal are natural casual speech.
opt_b: Saya tidak dapat hadir kerana ada urusan.
opt_b_why: This is correct but formal - it sounds stiff in a casual text.
opt_c_why: patik and tuanku belong to royal court language, not a chat with a friend.
opt_d_why: beta is the royal first person; it is not used in casual chat.
explanation: Casual register uses aku, tak, la; patik and beta belong to royal language.

prompt: When addressing royalty, the appropriate first-person pronoun is...
opt_a_why: Correct - patik is the humble first person used when speaking to royalty.
opt_b_why: aku is informal and would be disrespectful to royalty.
opt_c_why: saya is neutral and polite but not the special royal-address pronoun.
opt_d_why: beta is used BY royalty to refer to themselves, not by a commoner addressing them.
explanation: patik is used by a commoner addressing royalty; beta is used by the royal speaker.

prompt: Which sentence contains a register mismatch?
opt_a: Dalam laporan rasmi itu, dia tulis yang projek tu dah siap.
opt_a_why: Mistake - a formal report should not mix in colloquial tulis, tu and dah.
opt_b: Laporan rasmi itu menyatakan bahawa projek tersebut telah siap.
opt_b_why: This is consistently formal - no mismatch.
opt_c: Eh, projek tu dah siap ke belum?
opt_c_why: This is consistently casual - no mismatch.
opt_d: Projek tersebut telah disiapkan mengikut jadual.
opt_d_why: This is consistently formal - no mismatch.
explanation: Mixing colloquial tulis, tu and dah into a formal report is a register clash.

prompt: Rewrite this colloquial sentence in formal Malay: Saya tak tau dia nak datang ke tak.
accepted: saya tidak tahu sama ada dia hendak datang atau tidak
explanation: Formal Malay uses tidak, tahu, hendak and the construction sama ada... atau tidak.

prompt: Fill with the formal standard equivalent of the colloquial sebab: Kelas dibatalkan ___ guru sakit.
explanation: In formal register, kerana replaces the colloquial sebab for because.

prompt: Arrange into a formal sentence: tersebut / dimaklumkan / keputusan / kemudian / akan
explanation: Formal register uses tersebut and the di- passive dimaklumkan; the decision will be announced later.

prompt: Which word is the formal counterpart of the colloquial bagi tau?
opt_a_why: Correct - memberitahu is the standard verb for to tell or inform.
opt_b_why: bagi tau is itself the colloquial form, not the formal one.
opt_c_why: bgtau is a texting abbreviation, even less formal.
opt_d_why: This is colloquial and not a standard verb.
explanation: The colloquial bagi tau corresponds to the formal verb memberitahu.

prompt: Which sentence uses the question particle -kah correctly?
opt_a_why: Correct - -kah attaches to the fronted word benar to form a yes-no question.
opt_b_why: -kah should attach to the fronted focus word, here benar, not to pergi.
opt_c_why: -lah marks emphasis, not a question.
opt_d_why: pun means too or even; it does not form a question.
explanation: -kah attaches to the fronted focus word: Benarkah...? means Is it true that...?

prompt: In Dia pun tidak datang, what does pun express here?
opt_a: not even - it adds surprising inclusion
opt_a_why: Correct - pun with a negative gives the sense not even he came.
opt_b_why: pun does not soften requests; that is closer to -lah.
opt_c_why: questions use -kah, not pun.
opt_d_why: possession is shown by -nya or a following pronoun.
explanation: With a negative, pun means not even: Dia pun tidak datang - Not even he came.

prompt: Which sentence contains a mistake in particle use?
opt_a_why: Mistake - you cannot stack -kah and -lah on the same word; use Siapakah yang datang tadi?
opt_b_why: This is correct - -lah softens the command.
opt_c_why: This is correct - -kah forms the yes-no question on ada.
opt_d: Dia pun setuju dengan cadangan itu.
opt_d_why: This is correct - pun means too here.
explanation: -kah and -lah cannot be stacked on one word; choose one according to meaning.

prompt: Add the particle that turns this into a polite, softened command: Cuba___ baca ayat ini sekali lagi.
explanation: -lah softens the command: Cubalah baca... means Do try reading...

prompt: Arrange into a yes-no question: makan / kamu / sudah / adakah
explanation: -kah on ada forms the question word adakah; the sentence asks Have you eaten?

prompt: Choose the correct circumfix: the noun meaning the agreement reached between two parties, from setuju, is ___.
opt_a_why: Correct - per-...-an builds persetujuan, the mutual agreement reached between parties.
opt_b_why: kesetujuan is not standard; ke-...-an does not form this noun from setuju.
opt_c_why: penyetujuan is not a standard word; the process of approving is persetujuan or kelulusan.
opt_d_why: setujuan is not a word; a circumfix is needed, not a bare suffix.
explanation: per-...-an forms persetujuan - a mutual agreement reached between two sides.

prompt: From tani (to farm), the system or activity of farming as a whole is ___, while a single farmer is ___.
opt_a_why: Correct - per-...-an gives the activity pertanian (agriculture); peN- gives the doer petani (farmer).
opt_b_why: This reverses them; petani is the doer, pertanian is the activity.
opt_c_why: ketanian is not a standard word; the activity noun is pertanian.
opt_d_why: pertani is not a word; a farmer is petani.
explanation: per-...-an names a field or activity (pertanian); peN- names the doer (petani).

prompt: Which sentence uses the right circumfix for the meaning the difficulty he faced?
opt_a_why: Correct - ke-...-an on sukar gives the abstract noun kesukaran (difficulty).
opt_b_why: persukaran is not a standard word; the abstract noun is kesukaran.
opt_c_why: pesukaran is not a word; ke-...-an is needed here.
opt_d_why: sukaran is not a word; a circumfix is required, not a bare suffix.
explanation: ke-...-an forms abstract nouns of state from adjectives: sukar to kesukaran (difficulty).

prompt: Form the right circumfix noun from padu meaning unity: ___ rakyat menjamin keamanan negara.
explanation: From padu, per-...-an gives perpaduan (unity).

prompt: Translate to Malay using ke-...-an and pe-...-an: The cleanliness of the city depends on good rubbish management.
accepted: kebersihan bandar itu bergantung pada pengurusan sampah yang baik|kebersihan bandar bergantung kepada pengurusan sampah yang baik
explanation: kebersihan (cleanliness) uses ke-...-an; pengurusan (management) uses peN-...-an.

prompt: Arrange: kemerdekaan / negara / perjuangan / hasil / itu / ialah
accepted: kemerdekaan negara itu ialah hasil perjuangan
explanation: kemerdekaan (independence) uses ke-...-an from merdeka; perjuangan (struggle) uses per-...-an from juang.

prompt: Transform to the di- passive: Mereka akan membincangkan isu itu esok.
opt_a: Isu itu akan dibincangkan oleh mereka esok.
opt_a_why: Correct - akan stays before the di- verb dibincangkan, and oleh marks the agent.
opt_b: Isu itu dibincangkan akan oleh mereka esok.
opt_b_why: akan must precede the verb, not follow it.
opt_c: Isu itu akan membincangkan oleh mereka esok.
opt_c_why: membincangkan is the active verb; a passive needs dibincangkan.
opt_d: Mereka akan dibincangkan isu itu esok.
opt_d_why: This puts the agent in the subject slot - the roles are wrong.
explanation: In a di- passive the aspect marker akan stays before the verb: akan dibincangkan.

prompt: Transform to the pronoun-fronted passive: Saya sudah menghantar e-mel itu.
opt_a_why: Correct - pronoun agent, so me- is dropped and the aspect word sudah stays before the pronoun.
opt_b_why: With a pronoun agent, the natural form is the pronoun-fronted passive, not di- + bare pronoun.
opt_c_why: sudah belongs before the pronoun-verb unit: sudah saya hantar.
opt_d_why: This word order is ungrammatical.
explanation: Pronoun-fronted passive: object + aspect + pronoun + bare verb (E-mel itu sudah saya hantar).

prompt: Which sentence is correct when the agent is a first or second person pronoun?
opt_a: Surat itu akan saya balas esok.
opt_a_why: Correct - a pronoun agent triggers the pronoun-fronted passive; me- is dropped.
opt_b: Surat itu akan dibalas saya esok.
opt_b_why: di- + bare pronoun agent is dispreferred; use the pronoun-fronted passive.
opt_c: Surat itu saya akan balas esok.
opt_c_why: akan must come before the pronoun-verb unit: akan saya balas.
opt_d: Surat itu akan membalas saya esok.
opt_d_why: This is active and reverses the meaning.
explanation: With a pronoun agent, Malay prefers the pronoun-fronted passive over di- + bare pronoun.

prompt: Which sentence contains a passive-voice mistake?
opt_a: Tingkap itu kena pecahkan oleh budak nakal.
opt_a_why: Mistake - the kena passive does not take oleh; say kena pecahkan budak nakal or use the di- passive dipecahkan oleh.
opt_b: Projek itu telah disiapkan oleh pasukan kami.
opt_b_why: This is a correct di- passive with telah and oleh.
opt_c: Hadiah itu akan saya berikan kepadanya.
opt_c_why: This is a correct pronoun-fronted passive with akan.
opt_d: Pokok itu ditanam oleh datuk lima tahun lalu.
opt_d_why: This is a correct di- passive with oleh.
explanation: The kena passive normally does not use oleh; oleh belongs with the di- passive.

prompt: Choose the best passive for a formal report: The new policy was approved by the board.
opt_a: Dasar baharu itu telah diluluskan oleh lembaga.
opt_a_why: Correct - the di- passive with telah and oleh is the neutral, formal choice.
opt_b: Dasar baharu tu dah lulus oleh lembaga.
opt_b_why: tu and dah are colloquial; lulus without di- is not the passive verb.
opt_c: Dasar baharu itu kena lulus lembaga.
opt_c_why: kena gives an adversative, informal tone unsuitable for a formal report.
opt_d: Lembaga itu telah meluluskan oleh dasar baharu.
opt_d_why: meluluskan is active and oleh cannot follow it - the structure is wrong.
explanation: Formal reports use the di- passive: telah diluluskan oleh + agent.

prompt: Translate to Malay using the di- passive: This bridge has not yet been repaired by the authorities.
accepted: jambatan ini belum dibaiki oleh pihak berkuasa
explanation: belum (not yet) precedes the di- passive verb dibaiki; oleh marks the agent pihak berkuasa.

prompt: Transform this active sentence into the passive: Polis menahan suspek itu pagi tadi.
accepted: suspek itu ditahan oleh polis pagi tadi
explanation: The active menahan becomes the di- passive ditahan; the object suspek itu moves to subject position.

prompt: Arrange into a pronoun-fronted passive: kami / itu / sudah / selesaikan / tugas
explanation: Pronoun-fronted passive: object tugas itu, then sudah, then the pronoun-verb unit kami selesaikan.

prompt: Rewrite formally: Korang jangan lupa hantar kerja tu esok ya. Which is the best formal version?
opt_a: Anda semua jangan lupa menghantar kerja itu esok.
opt_a_why: Correct - anda semua, menghantar and itu are standard formal forms.
opt_b: Korang jangan lupa menghantar kerja itu esok.
opt_b_why: korang is colloquial; the formal form is anda semua or kamu semua.
opt_c: Anda semua jangan lupa hantar kerja tu esok ya.
opt_c_why: hantar without me-, tu and ya remain colloquial.
opt_d: Korang jangan lupa hantar kerja tu esok ya.
opt_d_why: This is still fully colloquial - nothing has been raised to formal register.
explanation: Formal register: anda semua for korang, menghantar for hantar, itu for tu, and drop the particle ya.

prompt: Which sentence mixes registers incorrectly?
opt_a: Pihak pengurusan ingin memaklumkan yang office akan tutup awal.
opt_a_why: Mistake - a formal notice should not mix English office; use pejabat with bahawa, not yang.
opt_b: Pihak pengurusan ingin memaklumkan bahawa pejabat akan ditutup awal.
opt_b_why: This is consistently formal - no mismatch.
opt_c: Eh, opis kita tutup awal hari ni la.
opt_c_why: This is consistently casual - no register clash.
opt_d: Pejabat akan ditutup lebih awal pada hari ini.
opt_d_why: This is consistently formal - no mismatch.
explanation: In a formal notice, avoid English insertions like office; use pejabat and the conjunction bahawa.

prompt: In a formal essay, which conjunction best replaces the colloquial tapi?
opt_a_why: Correct - tetapi is the standard formal form of the colloquial tapi.
opt_b_why: tapi is itself the colloquial short form, not the formal one.
opt_c_why: tp is a texting abbreviation, even less formal.
opt_d_why: cuma means only or just and shifts the meaning; it is not the direct equivalent.
explanation: The colloquial tapi corresponds to the formal conjunction tetapi (but).

prompt: Which reply is appropriately polite and formal in a job interview?
opt_a: Saya berharap dapat menyumbang kepada syarikat ini.
opt_a_why: Correct - saya, berharap and menyumbang form a polite, standard register.
opt_b: Aku harap boleh contribute kat company ni.
opt_b_why: aku, kat and English contribute are too casual for an interview.
opt_c: Patik amat berbesar hati menyertai syarikat ini.
opt_c_why: patik is royal-address language and is out of place in a job interview.
opt_d: Saya nak masuk company ni je la.
opt_d_why: nak, company, je and la are colloquial and sound flippant.
explanation: A job interview calls for polite standard Malay: saya, berharap, menyumbang - not royal patik nor casual aku.

prompt: Rewrite this formal sentence in natural colloquial Malay: Saya tidak dapat menghadiri majlis itu kerana saya sibuk.
accepted: saya tak boleh datang majlis tu sebab busy|aku tak boleh datang majlis tu sebab sibuk|saya tak dapat pergi majlis tu sebab sibuk
explanation: Colloquial Malay uses tak, tu and sebab; menghadiri becomes plain datang or pergi.

prompt: Fill with the formal verb replacing the colloquial bagi in: Sila ___ jawapan anda kepada panel.
explanation: In formal register kemukakan (to submit or put forward) replaces the colloquial bagi.

prompt: Which sentence stacks particles incorrectly?
opt_a_why: Mistake - tak datangpun wrongly fuses pun to a verb after negation; use tidak datang pun, and avoid overloading the clause with kah, pula and pun.
opt_b_why: This is correct - a single -kah question particle.
opt_c: Dia pun tidak hadir hari ini.
opt_c_why: This is correct - pun stands alone with the negative.
opt_d_why: This is correct - a single -lah on the command.
explanation: Particles like -kah, pun and pula should not be over-stacked; pun is written as a separate word.

prompt: What nuance does pula add in Dia pula yang marah?
opt_a: it expresses contrast or an unexpected turn
opt_a_why: Correct - pula marks a contrast or a surprising switch: now it is he who is angry.
opt_b_why: questions use -kah, not pula.
opt_c_why: negation uses tidak or bukan.
opt_d_why: Malay has no tense and pula does not mark it.
explanation: pula signals contrast or an unexpected turn - and now he is the one who is angry.

prompt: Which sentence uses the particle juga most naturally?
opt_a: Saya juga ingin menyertai program itu.
opt_a_why: Correct - juga means also or too and follows the subject naturally here.
opt_b: Juga saya ingin menyertai program itu.
opt_b_why: juga does not begin a sentence; it follows the element it includes.
opt_c: Saya ingin juga menyertai program itu juga.
opt_c_why: juga should not be doubled in one clause.
opt_d: Saya ingin menyertai juga program itu.
opt_d_why: juga is misplaced; it should follow the subject or the whole predicate, not split the verb phrase.
explanation: juga (also, too) normally follows the subject or the predicate it adds to.

prompt: Which sentence contains a particle error?
opt_a: Dia tak pergi sekolah lah semalam.
opt_a_why: Mistake - -lah does not attach mid-sentence to a time adverb here; -lah belongs on the focused or imperative word, e.g. Diapun tak pergi sekolah semalam or just drop lah.
opt_b_why: This is correct - -lah on an imperative verb.
opt_c_why: This is correct - -kah forms the question on the fronted mahal.
opt_d_why: This is correct - pun stands alone before the negative-aspect phrase.
explanation: -lah attaches to an imperative or a focused word, not loosely before a time adverb.

prompt: In Itulah jawapannya, what is the role of -lah?
opt_a: it gives emphatic focus - that is precisely the answer
opt_a_why: Correct - -lah on itu gives emphatic focus: that is exactly the answer.
opt_b: it turns the sentence into a question
opt_b_why: questions use -kah, not -lah.
opt_c_why: -lah does not mark number.
opt_d_why: possession is marked by -nya, as in jawapannya.
explanation: -lah gives emphatic focus: Itulah jawapannya means That is precisely the answer.

prompt: Translate to Malay using pun: Even the teacher did not know the answer.
accepted: guru pun tidak tahu jawapannya|guru itu pun tidak tahu jawapannya
explanation: pun after the subject gives the sense even: guru pun tidak tahu - even the teacher did not know.

prompt: Translate to Malay using -kah for a polite written question: Are you certain that this answer is correct?
accepted: pastikah anda bahawa jawapan ini betul|adakah anda pasti bahawa jawapan ini betul
explanation: -kah attaches to the fronted focus word pasti to form a polite written yes-no question.

prompt: Arrange into an emphatic sentence: dia / orang / membantu / yang / saya / lah
explanation: -lah on dia gives emphatic focus: Dialah orang yang membantu saya - He is the very one who helped me.

prompt: Which sentence places the particle -kah on the correct word for the intended focus question Is it TOMORROW the meeting will be held?
opt_a_why: Correct - to question the time, -kah attaches to the fronted focus word esok.
opt_b_why: This questions the meeting, not the time; -kah is on the wrong word.
opt_c_why: -kah must be on the fronted focus word esok, not on the verb at the end.
opt_d_why: -lah marks emphasis, not a question; the focus question needs -kah.
explanation: For a focus question, -kah attaches to the fronted constituent being queried: Esokkah...?

prompt: What is the difference between Makanlah and Makankah?
opt_a: Makanlah is an encouraging command (do eat); Makankah is a question (eat?)
opt_a_why: Correct - -lah forms the softened command, -kah forms the question.
opt_b: Both mean the same encouraging command
opt_b_why: They differ - one is a command, one is a question.
opt_c: Makanlah is a question; Makankah is a command
opt_c_why: This reverses them; -lah is the command, -kah is the question.
opt_d: Makankah marks the past tense of eat
opt_d_why: Malay has no tense; -kah marks a question.
explanation: -lah builds an encouraging command (Makanlah), -kah builds a question (Makankah).
